title: Per-context FIFO queue with concurrency limits

brief.why: Need sequential processing within a context (e.g. one email thread) but parallel processing across contexts. NanoClaw's GroupQueue pattern: per-group FIFO, global max of 3 concurrent agents, exponential backoff on failure.
brief.what: 1. Queue class: enqueue(contextId, triggerFn), drain(), shutdown() 2. Per-context ordering (FIFO within a context group) 3. Global concurrency limit (configurable, default 3) 4. Exponential backoff on spawn failure (5s base, max 5 retries) 5. Lane policy per context: followup (default), steer (interrupt), or collect (batch)
brief.done: Three triggers for different contexts process in parallel. Two triggers for the same context process sequentially. A spawn failure retries with backoff. Concurrency limit is respected.

steps: Queue class: enqueue(contextId, triggerFn), drain(), shutdown()
Per-context ordering (FIFO within a context group)
Global concurrency limit (configurable, default
4. Exponential backoff on spawn failure (5s base, max 5 retries)
Lane policy per context: followup (default), steer (interrupt), or collect (batch)

title: Eyesight filter: worker event stream to summary deposits

brief.why: The PM must not read full worker transcripts (context exhaustion). An eyesight filter distils each worker's Gueridon event stream into a per-session summary. The PM reads summaries, not transcripts. This is the deposit pattern applied to session monitoring.
brief.what: 1. Define summary schema: session name, status, key outputs, handoff path, error count 2. Build filter that consumes Gueridon session events and writes summary 3. Define granularity: per-session final summary vs periodic checkpoints 4. PM reads summaries via file deposit pattern
brief.done: Worker sessions produce summary deposits. PM reads them without exhausting context. Summary schema is documented and consistent.

title: CLAUDE.md for daemon-spawned agents

brief.why: Agents spawned by the daemon need context that interactive sessions get from the user: they're unattended, should use mise for email ops, follow bon conventions, write handoffs, and know their escalation path (HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED → Gueridon notification). Without this, daemon-spawned agents will behave like confused interactive sessions.
brief.what: 1. Section in project CLAUDE.md: 'When spawned by the daemon' 2. Covers: you are unattended, draft-only default, escalation via HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED, handoff writing is mandatory, use mise for all Google Workspace ops 3. Role-specific append via Agent SDK systemPrompt.append (email triage, heartbeat, worker, reflector)
brief.done: A daemon-spawned agent reading CLAUDE.md knows it's unattended, knows its escalation path, and writes a handoff on exit without being told.

done_note: Split into three actions: aby-josewe (aboyeur), aby-lizuno (PM), aby-nuripi (worker) — each tier needs its own CLAUDE.md

title: Systemd/launchd service wrapper

brief.why: The daemon needs to run persistently, survive reboots, and be manageable via standard OS tooling. NanoClaw ships both a launchd plist and systemd unit.
brief.what: 1. launchd plist for macOS (com.aboyeur.daemon.plist) 2. systemd unit for Linux (aboyeur-daemon.service) 3. Log rotation config 4. Graceful shutdown handler (SIGTERM drains queue, waits for active agents)
brief.done: launchctl load starts the daemon on macOS. systemctl --user start aboyeur starts it on Linux. Daemon survives reboots. Logs rotate.

title: Conductor mesh behaviour is fully characterised for CC integration

brief.why: We proved registration and messaging work, but several mesh behaviours are unknown and affect architecture decisions. Without answers, we risk building on assumptions that break at scale.
brief.what: 1. Message delivery when target is offline — queued or dropped? 2. Multiple agents same account — routing semantics 3. Peer list divergence across clients (confirmed: each session gets replay from own connect time) 4. Input collision: incoming mesh message while user is typing lands as [Pasted Text] marker in CC input box (does not submit, but disrupts typing) 5. Event replay semantics on reconnection 6. Token refresh behaviour under long-lived connections
brief.done: Each question answered with evidence (tested or documented). Answers captured in CONDUCTOR-PROTOCOL.md.

title: The explore-decide-plan-execute cycle runs with minimal human gates

brief.why: Today the human (Sameer) steers the full cycle: commissions research, relays between Claudes, challenges assumptions, decides direction, transitions to planning. Most of this is mechanical or already covered by the reflector pattern. Encoding it means Aboyeur can run multi-session projects with human involvement only at irreversible decision points.
brief.what: 1. Explore-phase prompts (worker: research options; reflector: challenge assumptions and find gaps) 2. READY-TO-PLAN gate criteria (no unexplained data flows, irreversible decisions made by human, prior work surfaced) 3. Plan-phase prompts (worker: convert direction to Bons; reflector: check Bons against research) 4. Decision heuristics for unattended operation (reversible=proceed, irreversible/expensive=HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED) 5. Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups
brief.done: Aboyeur can take a desired outcome, commission research across sessions, challenge its own assumptions, present decision points to human, convert approved direction to Bons, and begin execution — with human only needed for go/no-go on irreversible choices.

done_note: Actions redistributed to aby-titugu (aboyeur, decision heuristics) and aby-nafola (PM, explore/plan prompts, garde integration). The cycle is now the PM's behaviour, not a standalone concern.

title: CC instances are first-class agents on the Anthropic conductor mesh

brief.why: The pivot.claude.ai bundle contains a production conductor protocol (peer-to-peer agent mesh via WebSocket). The claude-in-office session (Mar 14 2026) mapped the full wire protocol and designed a filesystem-based adaptation for CC instances. Guéridon bridge is the natural home — it already manages multiple CC processes, push notifications, and mobile/desktop fluidity. Need to read Guéridon codebase properly and design the bridge integration.
brief.what: 1. Get OAuth token via api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/profile (token from Office add-in localStorage, or via OAuth flow). 2. Connect WebSocket to wss://bridge.claudeusercontent.com/v2/conductor/{profile.uuid}. 3. Send register message with agentId, schema, oauth_token. 4. Build as MCP server providing get_connected_agents, send_message tools. 5. Handle incoming conductor_message → inject into CC conversation. 6. Handle file sharing via conductor protocol. 7. The conductor URL path and registration format are confirmed from bundle analysis (line 167222, 68774). Server accepts non-browser WebSocket connections (no CORS). Key blocker: stable OAuth token refresh.
brief.done: CC agent visible to Office agents on the conductor mesh, able to send and receive messages

title: Mesh injection works in autonomous (unattended) mode

brief.why: Gueridon-spawned sessions have no human typing, so last_key_was_enter is never true and USER_QUIET_MIN never triggers. Mesh messages pile up in inbox forever. Breaks the daemon architecture where aboyeur sends instructions to PMs via mesh.
brief.what: 1. Add --unattended flag to claude-mesh that sets last_key_was_enter=True and USER_QUIET_MIN=0 from startup 2. Gueridon spawn pattern passes --unattended automatically 3. Test: prompt-idle gating with attended vs unattended modes 4. Test: verify prompt character detection survives CC version changes (fallback if no prompt seen within 30s)
brief.done: An unattended claude-mesh session receives and auto-submits mesh messages without human intervention. Attended sessions still gate on 10s quiet + enter.

title: CC session integration — hook or MCP wiring inbox/outbox to live session

brief.why: The bridge writes to files but CC sessions have no way to know a message arrived. Without injection, the human must relay messages manually. The UserPromptSubmit hook pattern (like bon tactical) is the proven approach.
brief.what: 1. UserPromptSubmit hook checks inbox.jsonl for unread messages 2. Cursor tracking so messages appear once 3. Outbox helper — CC can send via a simple command rather than raw echo to jsonl 4. Consider MCP server as alternative (provides tools, but needs session restart)
brief.done: A CC session with the hook installed sees inbound mesh messages automatically on the next prompt. Can send replies without manual JSON construction.

steps: Switch mesh-claude.py sidecar from Python bridge to TS bridge (npx tsx)
Test full mesh round-trip: Alice sends → Bob receives via PTY → Bob replies → Alice gets reply
Retire tools/conductor-bridge.py (or mark deprecated)

title: OAuth credentials for claude@planetmodha.com

brief.why: Need a separate Google Cloud OAuth client under planetmodha.com that the daemon uses to mint tokens for claude@planetmodha.com. This gives the daemon (and only the daemon's mise instance) access to that mailbox with send permissions. The ITV mise instance never gets send capability — enforced by separate OAuth clients, not just config.
brief.what: 1. Create OAuth client in planetmodha.com Google Cloud project 2. Configure with gmail.modify scope (includes send) 3. Daemon uses this client to mint/refresh tokens for claude@planetmodha.com 4. Store credentials separate from ITV OAuth client 5. Verify: daemon can list and send messages for claude@planetmodha.com
brief.done: jeton get-token --account claude@planetmodha.com returns a valid OAuth token with gmail.modify scope. Token refresh works unattended.

waiting_for: Sameer creates OAuth client in planetmodha.com Google Cloud project and runs initial token mint

title: Daemon watches the world and wakes the aboyeur

brief.why: The always-on Node.js process is the lizard brain — it polls trigger sources (Gmail, cron, filesystem), normalises them into a SQLite queue, and spawns the aboyeur when something needs attention. No intelligence, just plumbing. This is the foundation everything else sits on.
brief.what: 1. Gmail poll trigger via direct API (aby-sanimu) 2. HEARTBEAT cron trigger — also checks if aboyeur is alive (aby-vemapa) 3. OAuth credentials for planetmodha.com (aby-hemimi) 4. Systemd/launchd service wrapper (aby-dagofi)
brief.done: Daemon runs as a service. Gmail and HEARTBEAT triggers fire reliably. OAuth tokens mint and refresh. Daemon restarts the aboyeur if it dies.

title: Garde-manger integration for prior work discovery

brief.why: In this conversation, Sameer had to manually supply the earlier email infrastructure research. An explore reflector should search garde-manger for 'have we explored this before?' and surface relevant prior sessions, preventing re-discovery of settled questions.
brief.what: 1. Add garde-manger search step to explore-reflector prompt 2. Search patterns: topic keywords, repo names, tool names mentioned in the exploration 3. Surface relevant handoffs and session summaries as context 4. Reflector notes: 'Prior session on [date] concluded [X] — incorporate or revisit?'
brief.done: An explore-reflector reviewing a topic we've previously explored finds and cites the prior session. Prior conclusions are incorporated rather than re-derived.

title: Pre-compaction memory flush hook

brief.why: OpenClaw's best pattern: before context compaction, a silent turn fires where the agent saves critical state to external files. This prevents the gradual context erosion that degrades long-running agents. The Agent SDK supports PreCompact hooks.
brief.what: 1. PreCompact hook in Claude Code settings.json that triggers agent to write current state to handoff file 2. State includes: what was being worked on, what's done, what's blocked, key decisions made 3. Hook fires before CC's internal summarization 4. Handoff file written to standard location (~/.claude/handoffs/) 5. No Agent SDK required — CC hooks work for any spawned claude process
brief.done: An agent session that runs long enough to trigger compaction has its critical state preserved in a handoff file. A new session reading that handoff can continue without asking 'what was I doing?'

title: Distilled Aboyeur pattern from real session data

brief.why: The claude-in-office session (Mar 14 2026) analysed 1,012 handoff files and found the Aboyeur pattern has clear lineage from Dec 2025. 60% of handoffs contain reflections. The pattern is: review while context is rich, act immediately, track what cant be done now with consequences. Two communication channels: bridge injection (fake user input for authority) and peer (conductor for honest exchange). Bon outcome completion is the natural trigger. This needs distilling into a design doc so the next Claude working here has the full picture.
brief.what: 1. Document the six review questions (from /close Orient). 2. Document the anti-deferral heuristic (do it now or bon it, no third option). 3. Document the two channels (bridge injection vs peer) and when to use each. 4. Document the bon outcome completion trigger. 5. Document the fresh-context advantage for honest critique. 6. Address sycophancy risk. 7. Write as understanding.md contribution or design doc.
brief.done: Design document captures the full Aboyeur pattern with real examples from handoff data, ready for implementation

done_note: understanding.md co-written by two CC sessions via conductor mesh. Captures the six review questions, anti-deferral heuristic, two channels, fresh-context advantage, accomplishment trigger, three output buckets.

title: Regroup bon outcomes along software architecture boundaries

brief.why: Current outcomes are grouped by feature/capability (triggers, email, planning). But session efficiency depends on context correlation — Claudes should be able to work an outcome without loading unrelated modules. Outcomes should mirror code boundaries.
brief.what: 1. Map current src/ modules to functional groups 2. Review existing outcomes against module groups 3. Propose regrouped outcomes that align code and plan 4. Execute regrouping with bon convert/edit
brief.done: Each outcome's actions touch at most 2-3 related modules. A Claude can work any outcome without reading the whole codebase.

done_note: Executing now — regrouping bons under new architecture-aligned outcomes

title: Aboyeur CLAUDE.md — goals, routing rules, promotion detection

brief.why: The aboyeur needs a system prompt that encodes: read bon outcomes (not actions), route triggers as one-shot or PM, detect when a one-shot has created bons (promotion), assign session names, consume minimal context. This is a different document from the PM or worker CLAUDE.md.
brief.what: 1. Draft aboyeur system prompt with routing rules 2. Goal-reading pattern: bon list --json filtered to outcomes only 3. Promotion detection: monitor for new bons created by one-shots 4. Session naming template injection
brief.done: Aboyeur CLAUDE.md exists. An aboyeur Claude can read it cold and correctly route a trigger as one-shot or PM.

title: Conductor prompt encodes session lifecycle discipline

brief.why: Daemon-spawned Claudes currently get no guidance on mid-session tactics (context correlation, three questions, wind-down timing). These patterns are proven effective but live only in memory files that workers don't read.
brief.what: 1. Distill session-lifecycle-patterns.md into conductor system prompt sections 2. Add three-questions checkpoint to worker prompts at natural pauses 3. Add context-correlation hint to conductor's next-action selection
brief.done: Conductor prompt includes lifecycle sections. Workers pause for three questions. Conductor picks next work by context overlap.

done_note: Subsumed by aby-lizuno (PM CLAUDE.md encodes human-simulator patterns) — same scope, better framing

title: Build spawnAgent() primitive

brief.why: Everything else depends on a clean spawn function. Gueridon's bridge.ts already does this: env-var stripping, stdio JSON streaming, session resume, orphan tracking. Extract that pattern into a reusable function rather than introducing the Agent SDK as a new dependency.
brief.what: 1. Extract spawnAgent(folder, prompt, sessionId?, options?) from Gueridon's spawnCC() pattern 2. Env-var stripping (CLAUDECODE + CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT) 3. claude CLI with --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json 4. Session resume via --session-id / --resume flags 5. Stdout event parsing (reuse Gueridon's event handling) 6. Configurable max_turns via --max-turns flag 7. Return type: {status, result, newSessionId}
brief.done: Can spawn a Claude session from a script, pass it a prompt, get structured output back. Session resume works across invocations. Process exits cleanly. No Agent SDK dependency — uses claude CLI directly.

steps: Extract spawnAgent(folder, prompt, sessionId?, options?) from Gueridon's spawnCC() pattern
Env-var stripping (CLAUDECODE + CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT)
claude CLI with --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json
Session resume via --session-id / --resume flags
Stdout event parsing (reuse Gueridon's event handling)
Configurable max_turns via --max-turns flag
Return type: {status, result, newSessionId}

title: Multi-session workflow is encoded and self-reinforcing

brief.why: Session lifecycle patterns are currently tribal knowledge in Sameer's head and memory files. Daemon-spawned Claudes won't inherit discipline unless it's in their prompts and the plan structure mirrors the code.
brief.what: 1. Encode session lifecycle patterns into conductor/daemon prompts 2. Regroup bons along software architecture boundaries 3. Engineer feedback loops as first-class planning step for each new outcome
brief.done: Conductor-spawned Claudes naturally follow session patterns. Bon outcomes map to code modules. New outcomes include feedback loop design.

title: Promotion pattern: one-shot escalates to PM when bons are created

brief.why: A one-shot email conversation can develop into a multi-session project. When the one-shot creates bon actions under an outcome, the aboyeur needs to detect this and spawn a PM to take over. This is the GTD pattern: a next action reveals itself to be a project.
brief.what: 1. Define the promotion signal (new bons created by a session that started as one-shot) 2. Aboyeur monitors bon state after one-shot completes 3. If new outcome+actions exist, spawn PM scoped to that outcome 4. Hand off the one-shot's session context (handoff file) to the PM
brief.done: A one-shot that creates bons triggers PM spawning. The PM picks up from the one-shot's handoff. No manual intervention needed.

title: Bridge broadcasts session status and availability to mesh peers

brief.why: Without status, peers cannot tell if a session is busy or available. This causes wasted messages and the human-as-hook problem. Office agents already broadcast fileName and status — CC agents should too. A cleared busy flag with unread inbox messages is a natural daemon trigger.
brief.what: 1. Poll a working_on file in agent dir — emit status event on change 2. Busy flag file — exists means head-down, absent means available 3. Emit {type: status, fileName: contents, _agent_id: id} on the WebSocket 4. Integrate busy/inbox check as daemon trigger signal
brief.done: Peers see what a CC session is working on via get_connected_agents. Busy flag suppresses non-urgent messages. Busy-clear + unread inbox triggers daemon wake.

title: Mesh sessions are immune to CC survey prompts

brief.why: CC occasionally shows a 'How is Claude doing?' satisfaction prompt. In mesh context, an incoming message could land on it and submit garbage feedback, or the survey could intercept an injected message meant as a new prompt. Breaks autonomous operation.
brief.what: 1. Detect survey prompt in CC output within mesh-claude.py PTY read loop 2. Either auto-dismiss or suppress message injection while survey is active 3. Test: inject a mesh message while survey is showing
brief.done: Mesh messages never interact with CC survey prompts. Survey is either auto-dismissed or injection is paused until it clears.

title: Planning reflector prompt

brief.why: Current reflector-open.md reviews code and work output. A planning reflector reviews architecture and design — challenging assumptions, walking through data flows, finding hidden decisions. This is what caught the 'mise polling needs a Claude' mistake.
brief.what: 1. New prompt file: shared/prompts/planning-reflector.md 2. Walk-through-every-data-flow section 3. Challenge-the-layering section 4. Find-hidden-decisions section 5. READY-TO-PLAN gate criteria 6. Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups
brief.done: Planning reflector catches at least one non-obvious assumption error when run against the daemon's own architecture. The READY-TO-PLAN gate produces a clear summary of decisions made and risks remaining.

steps: New prompt file: shared/prompts/planning-reflector.md
Walk-through-every-data-flow section
Challenge-the-layering section
Find-hidden-decisions section
READY-TO-PLAN gate criteria
Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups

title: SQLite trigger table and polling loop

brief.why: Triggers from different sources (Gmail, cron, filesystem) need dedup, cursor tracking, and crash recovery. SQLite is the proven pattern — NanoClaw uses it, it survives process restarts, and it's queryable for debugging.
brief.what: 1. Schema: triggers(id, source, context_group, payload, status, created_at, claimed_at) 2. Polling loop (2s interval) drains pending triggers 3. Cursor tracking per source (last_seen timestamp) 4. Dedup by source+payload hash 5. Crash recovery: unclaimed triggers from before last startup get re-queued
brief.done: Triggers inserted by any source appear in the queue and get processed exactly once. After a crash, pending triggers are recovered on restart.

steps: Schema: triggers(id, source, context_group, payload, status, created_at, claimed_at)
Polling loop (2s interval) drains pending triggers
Cursor tracking per source (last_seen timestamp)
Dedup by source+payload hash
Crash recovery: unclaimed triggers from before last startup get re-queued

title: PM CLAUDE.md encodes human-simulator patterns

brief.why: The PM needs a system prompt that encodes Sameer's operational patterns: read bon --json for one project, manage the beat (work → review → route), apply context correlation for next-action, ask three questions at checkpoints, wind down at 20-25% context, report progress one-liners to aboyeur. This is the richest of the three CLAUDE.md documents.
brief.what: 1. Draft PM system prompt with beat pattern 2. Context correlation heuristic section 3. Three questions checkpoint protocol 4. Wind-down and restart-from-bon-state protocol 5. Eyesight filter reading protocol — how PM reads worker summaries
brief.done: PM CLAUDE.md exists. A PM Claude can read it cold and manage a sequence of workers/reflectors for one project using bon --json.

title: Decision heuristics for unattended operation

brief.why: When the human is unavailable, the Aboyeur Claude needs rules for when to proceed vs when to wait. Reversible and cheap = proceed. Irreversible or expensive = HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED. These heuristics encode the judgment calls the human would make.
brief.what: 1. Decision matrix in shared/prompts/decision-heuristics.md 2. Axes: reversibility (easy/hard/impossible), cost (cheap/moderate/expensive), novelty (proven pattern/new approach), blast radius (local/shared/public) 3. Rules: proceed if reversible+cheap+proven; escalate if any axis is red; default to escalate when uncertain 4. Integration point: reflector prompts reference the heuristics when evaluating 'should we proceed or ask?'
brief.done: An Aboyeur reflector facing a reversible cheap decision proceeds without human input. An Aboyeur reflector facing an irreversible decision writes HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED with the specific decision to be made. No false negatives on irreversible decisions in testing.

title: Add send operation to mise-en-space

brief.why: The daemon's mise instance needs send capability for claude@planetmodha.com. But this codepath must NEVER exist in the ITV mise installation. Options: git branch that only the daemon deploys from, a feature flag gated by OAuth client identity, or a fork. The separation must be structural (code doesn't exist) not just config (code exists but is disabled).
brief.what: 1. New tool: tools/send.py wrapping gmail.users.drafts().send() 2. Add 'send' to OPERATIONS frozenset and _DISPATCH dict in server.py 3. This code lives ONLY in the daemon's mise deployment — not in the ITV branch 4. Git strategy: daemon branch of mise-en-space that carries send.py, rebased onto main. Or: mise loads tools from a plugin directory, send.py lives outside the repo. 5. Tests (in daemon branch only)
brief.done: mcp__mise__do(operation='send', draft_id='...') sends a draft. Without --allow-send config, the operation returns an error explaining it's disabled. Tests pass.

title: Inter-Claude comms trial in tmux

brief.why: We designed a filesystem-based conductor protocol for CC-to-CC peer communication (inspired by Anthropic's pivot.claude.ai conductor). Need a lightweight proof-of-concept: two CC sessions in tmux, shared directory, one concrete task, meta-reflection prompts to capture the experience of inter-Claude communication. The claude-in-office session (Mar 14 2026) produced the full protocol design.
brief.what: 1. Create shared directory convention (~/.claude-conductor/agents/). 2. Write minimal prompt snippets teaching each Claude inbox checking and message sending. 3. Pick a concrete task (eg one Claude writes analysis, another reviews). 4. Add interstitial meta-reflection prompt so participating Claudes capture their experience of peer communication. 5. Run the trial, gather reflections.
brief.done: Two Claudes successfully exchanged messages via filesystem, completed a task collaboratively, and produced meta-reflections on the experience

done_note: Superseded by conductor mesh proof-of-concept. Two CC sessions exchanged messages bidirectionally through Anthropic's production bridge alongside Office agents. More powerful than the tmux/filesystem trial — real infrastructure, not a simulation.

title: Sidecar health monitor — auto-restart dead bridge

brief.why: Bridge can crash (e.g. unhandled 'Agent not found' error) and mesh-claude.py doesn't notice. Status file lies, outbox writes silently succeed but nothing goes over the wire. Discovered during aby-dawugu characterisation when our own bridge was dead for 10+ minutes undetected.
brief.what: 1. mesh-claude.py polls bridge process liveness in the main I/O loop (proc.poll()). 2. If bridge dies, update status file to 'disconnected'. 3. Auto-relaunch with backoff. 4. Inject a mesh status line into PTY on crash/recovery so CC knows.
brief.done: Bridge crash is detected within 1 poll cycle (0.5s), status file updated, bridge relaunched, CC informed via PTY injection.

title: Conductor mesh trigger source in daemon poll loop

brief.why: The conductor mesh is a new trigger source alongside Gmail and cron. Without it, the daemon cannot wake agents in response to peer messages. The mesh bridge writes to inbox.jsonl which the daemon can poll just like other trigger sources.
brief.what: 1. Daemon polls conductor bridge inbox files for new messages 2. New message becomes a trigger row (source=conductor, context_group from sender/topic) 3. Integrate with existing trigger loop and context queue
brief.done: A conductor mesh message creates a trigger row and spawns an agent within one poll cycle.

steps: Daemon polls conductor bridge inbox files for new messages
New message becomes a trigger row (source=conductor, context_group from sender/topic)
Integrate with existing trigger loop and context queue

title: PM Claudes manage multi-session projects as human simulators

brief.why: The PM Claude is the middle manager — scoped to one project, deep in the weeds, managing the beat pattern (work → review → route). It reads bon state via --json, spawns workers and reflectors via Gueridon bridge, monitors them through filtered event summaries, and applies session lifecycle patterns (context correlation, three questions, drift detection). Disposable — restarts from bon state.
brief.what: 1. PM CLAUDE.md encodes human-sim patterns (beat, three questions, wind-down) 2. Gueridon bridge integration for spawning/monitoring workers (from aby-pinida) 3. Eyesight filter: worker event stream → per-session summary deposits 4. Context correlation heuristic for next-action selection 5. Explore-phase and planning-phase prompt pairs (from aby-wujime) 6. Garde-manger integration for prior work discovery (from aby-hikifa) 7. Context-pressure reflector trigger (from aby-vaniho)
brief.done: PM manages a sequence of 5-10 worker/reflector sessions for one project. Uses bon --json as primary state. Eyesight filter prevents context exhaustion. Reports progress one-liners to aboyeur. Restartable from bon state.

title: Feedback loop design is a first-class planning step

brief.why: Tight feedback loops (push-run-log-introspect-rewrite) are the single biggest multiplier for dev velocity. But they're never planned explicitly — they emerge by accident or after friction. Making loop design a standard step when planning new outcomes prevents wasted sessions.
brief.what: 1. Add feedback-loop-design prompt to planning reflector 2. Template: what's the inner loop, what's the cycle time target, what gates need removing 3. Apply retroactively to current outcomes (what loops exist, what's missing)
brief.done: Planning reflector asks about feedback loops. Each outcome has a documented inner loop or explicit note that none is needed.

title: Document cross-machine collaboration protocol

brief.why: Today's two-Claude session discovered conventions for working the same codebase from different machines via mesh (push notification before git push, file ownership boundaries, bon action-level only, cross-review protocol). These are only in the mesh transcript — next time this happens, the conventions will be re-derived from scratch.
brief.what: 1. Write a protocol doc in docs/ covering push/pull sequencing, file ownership to avoid conflicts, bon cross-machine constraints, mesh communication conventions. 2. Reference today's session findings.
brief.done: A future two-Claude session can follow the protocol without re-deriving conventions.

title: Structured reflector verdict schema

brief.why: Reflectors currently write prose handoffs. The PM needs structured JSON verdicts to route automatically: approved → next action, rejected → fix session, escalate → email Sameer. Without structure, the PM has to read and interpret prose, which costs context and introduces ambiguity.
brief.what: 1. Define verdict JSON schema: {approved, issues[], confidence, recommendation} 2. Update reflector-open.md to emit verdict as a structured block 3. Define where verdict is deposited (alongside handoff) 4. PM parsing convention for verdict files
brief.done: Reflectors emit JSON verdicts. PM can parse them without reading the full reflector session. Routing decisions are automatic based on verdict.

title: Mesh awareness skill for CC sessions

brief.why: Every new mesh-claude session starts cold — Claude doesn't know mesh commands, peer etiquette, or how to restart a dead bridge. shared/prompts/mesh-awareness.md exists but nothing loads it as a skill. Without a skill, Claudes either ignore mesh messages or fumble the CLI.
brief.what: 1. Create a mesh skill in claude-config skills/ that loads mesh-awareness.md context. 2. Include bridge restart procedure, peer list interpretation, message serialisation safety. 3. Consider auto-loading when MESH_AGENT_ID env var is set.
brief.done: A Claude launched via claude-mesh can use mesh commands correctly without prior explanation. Skill triggers on mesh-related queries.

title: Worker CLAUDE.md conventions

brief.why: Workers need a CLAUDE.md that makes them productive without knowing they're orchestrated. It should encode: how to read bons, how to write handoffs in a PM-parseable format, session naming in artifacts, how to signal completion. Must NOT reference the aboyeur, PM, or orchestration.
brief.what: 1. Draft worker CLAUDE.md template 2. Handoff format that PM can parse (structured sections) 3. Session name injection via --append-system-prompt 4. Completion signal convention
brief.done: Worker CLAUDE.md exists. A worker Claude reads it and produces handoffs the PM can parse without reading the full session.

title: Spawn-on-message — daemon treats inbound mesh messages as triggers

brief.why: The mesh enables a new trigger type: a message arrives for a repo with no active session. Without spawn-on-message, the message is lost or waits indefinitely. This is the event-driven Aboyeur — wakes on trigger, acts, sleeps.
brief.what: 1. Gueridon registers repo slots on the mesh 2. Incoming conductor_message for an empty slot becomes a trigger row (source=conductor, context_group=repo_path) 3. Daemon spawns a session with the message as initial prompt 4. If session already active, route message to it via hook injection
brief.done: A mesh message to an unoccupied repo spawns a fresh CC session within 10 seconds. Message to an occupied repo appears in the active session.

title: Bridge script handles reconnection, token refresh, and dedup

brief.why: Current bridge script is a proof-of-concept with known bugs. Token expires every 24h and the bridge reads it once at startup. Without refresh handling, long-running bridges silently fail.
brief.what: 1. Re-read token from .credentials.json on reconnect 2. Detect 401/auth errors and trigger re-read 3. Outbox cursor persisted across reconnects (done) 4. Inbound message dedup across reconnects (done)
brief.done: Bridge survives 48+ hours including at least one token refresh cycle. No duplicate messages in inbox after reconnection.

steps: Re-read token on reconnect (from Keychain on macOS, file on Linux)
Detect 401/auth errors from WebSocket and profile API, trigger fresh token read
Test: simulate token expiry by reconnecting after delay

title: Conductor spawns workers via Gueridon bridge

brief.why: The conductor is a human simulator — it does what Sameer does, automated. It needs to spawn and monitor worker Claudes at one remove, the same way Sameer uses Gueridon from his phone. Using spawnAgent() directly makes the conductor a process manager; using Gueridon bridge makes it a session director. Workers stay oblivious to orchestration — they just see a directory, CLAUDE.md, and bons.
brief.what: 1. Expose Gueridon bridge as MCP tools the conductor can call (spawn session, list sessions, kill session, get session events) 2. Build eyesight filter: stream worker events, deposit per-session summaries (not full transcripts) 3. Conductor reads bon state via bon list --json, picks next action by context correlation 4. Conductor monitors workers via filtered event deposits, asks three questions at checkpoints 5. Structured reflector verdicts (approved/rejected/escalate) route conductor decisions
brief.done: Conductor spawns workers via Gueridon bridge API. Worker event stream is filtered to summaries the conductor reads without context exhaustion. Conductor uses bon --json for routing decisions. Reflector verdicts are structured JSON, not just handoff prose.

title: Email flows in and out without human involvement

brief.why: Email is both the primary trigger (things arrive) and the escalation channel (things need human attention). The daemon polls Gmail, one-shots triage simple emails, PMs handle complex threads. Drafts by default, send only from planetmodha.com deployment. This is the service hatch between the kitchen and the outside world.
brief.what: 1. Email triage prompt template and thread context (from aby-ravano) 2. Add send operation to mise-en-space (from aby-mideti) 3. Escalation drafts — PM or aboyeur can draft 'needs your attention' emails to Sameer
brief.done: Emails trigger appropriate handling (one-shot or PM). Triage produces correct drafts. Send operation exists in daemon deployment only. Escalation emails reach Sameer when needed.

title: Escalation drafts — aboyeur or PM can email Sameer when stuck

brief.why: When a PM hits an escalation verdict or the aboyeur detects a stuck PM, the system needs to draft an email to Sameer explaining what happened and what decision is needed. This is the human-in-the-loop escape valve.
brief.what: 1. Define escalation trigger conditions (reflector escalate verdict, PM stuck, repeated failures) 2. Escalation email template: what happened, what was tried, what decision is needed 3. Wire mise do(operation='draft') into PM and aboyeur escalation paths
brief.done: Stuck situations produce a draft email in Sameer's Gmail. The draft includes enough context for Sameer to make a decision without opening a Claude session.

title: One-shot vs PM routing logic

brief.why: The aboyeur's core job is routing: given a trigger, decide whether to spawn a one-shot Claude (single-session, returns summary) or a PM Claude (multi-session, manages a project). Without this logic, every trigger gets the same treatment, wasting PM overhead on simple tasks or under-serving complex ones.
brief.what: 1. Routing heuristics: does the trigger reference existing bons? Is the email a 'go' signal or a question? 2. One-shot spawn pattern: session name, working dir, prompt template, summary return 3. PM spawn pattern: session name, project dir, PM CLAUDE.md, initial prompt with trigger payload 4. Fallback: if uncertain, spawn one-shot with promotion capability
brief.done: Aboyeur correctly routes simple triggers to one-shots and project triggers to PMs. Uncertain triggers default to one-shot with promotion.

title: Email triage prompt template and thread context

brief.why: The agent spawned by the Gmail trigger needs a prompt that orients it: who sent the email, what's the thread history, what actions are available (reply draft, escalate, archive, ignore). Thread context across sessions needs per-thread handoff files.
brief.what: 1. Prompt template: shared/prompts/email-triage.md 2. Classification categories: reply (draft), escalate (notify human), archive (no action), defer (add to reading queue) 3. Per-thread handoff file pattern: handoffs/email/{thread_id}.md 4. Thread context loading: agent reads thread handoff + fetches recent messages via mise 5. Escalation path: write HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED to handoff, daemon notifies via Gueridon push
brief.done: An email from a known contact gets a draft reply. An email requesting a decision gets escalated with a notification. A newsletter gets archived with a one-line summary in the thread handoff. All three paths tested.

title: Test and document mesh edge behaviours

brief.why: Eight open questions from the spike plus a peer-list divergence finding from today. Answers determine architecture decisions for daemon triggers, reconnection, and multi-agent spawning.
brief.what: 1. Offline message delivery — send to disconnected peer, reconnect, check. 2. Multiple agents per profile — spawn 3+ bridges simultaneously. 3. Graceful deregistration — close WS cleanly, check peer events. 4. Custom agentId format — register with non-standard ID. 5. Rate limits on send_message — burst 50 messages. 6. Capture peer-list divergence finding. 7. Update CONDUCTOR-PROTOCOL.md with all findings.
brief.done: Each question answered with test evidence. CONDUCTOR-PROTOCOL.md updated with findings section.

steps: Offline message delivery — send to disconnected peer, reconnect, check.
Multiple agents per profile — spawn 3+ bridges simultaneously.
Graceful deregistration — close WS cleanly, check peer events.
Custom agentId format — register with non-standard ID.
Rate limits on send_message — burst 50 messages.
Capture peer-list divergence finding.
Update CONDUCTOR-PROTOCOL.md with all findings.

title: Gmail poll trigger via direct API

brief.why: The daemon polls Gmail directly (not via mise MCP, which requires a Claude session). Jeton provides OAuth tokens. Gmail API returns message list with minimal overhead. Mise is for the agent's rich operations (fetch, draft, reply), not for the daemon's heartbeat check.
brief.what: 1. Gmail API client using jeton OAuth token 2. Poll: users.messages.list(q='is:unread newer_than:5m') every 60s 3. For each new message: insert trigger row (source=gmail, context_group=thread_id, payload={messageId, subject, from}) 4. Cursor tracking via Gmail historyId (more reliable than timestamp)
brief.done: New email to claude@planetmodha.com creates a trigger row within 60 seconds. Duplicate emails are not re-triggered. Token refresh works unattended.

title: Mesh outbox is safe against shell newline injection

brief.why: When CC writes to outbox.jsonl via Bash echo (rather than the mesh send CLI), zsh interprets backslash-n as literal newlines, splitting JSON across multiple lines. The mesh send CLI already serialises safely via json.dumps — the bug is at the CC-to-bash boundary, not in mesh-claude.py itself.
brief.what: 1. Document in mesh-awareness.md that CC must use python3 json.dumps (not echo) for outbox writes 2. Consider a mesh-send wrapper script that CC can call safely from Bash 3. Test: multi-line message round-trips through outbox correctly
brief.done: A message containing newlines in the body arrives at the recipient intact. No malformed lines in outbox.jsonl.

title: Excel Librarian uses Custom XML Parts for persistent document-scoped storage

brief.why: The Excel Librarian needs state that survives across conversations (loaded skills, config, conversation history, work tracking). Currently no persistence mechanism beyond visible sheet cells. Custom XML Parts confirmed working via live Office Claude interrogation (2026-03-15) — they persist with the workbook, are hidden from users, support namespace-based queries via getByNamespace(), and carry arbitrary XML. This also enables bon-on-Office: work tracking items stored as Custom XML Parts travel with the document invisibly, removing the need for a dedicated Librarian sheet or conductor messaging for local tracking.
brief.what: 1. Spike context.workbook.customXmlParts API — write and read back arbitrary XML in Office.js 2. Design namespace schema: urn:librarian:state for config/skills, urn:bon:items for work tracking 3. Implement read-on-open and write-on-close lifecycle via execute_office_js 4. Test persistence across save/reopen cycles and across different machines (OneDrive sync) 5. Migrate Librarian canonical state from visible Protocol/Decisions sheets to Custom XML Parts (keep Protocol sheet as human-readable cold-start guide) 6. Build bon skill for claude.ai that includes execute_office_js snippets for Custom XML Part CRUD with urn:bon:items namespace
brief.done: Excel Librarian recovers state between conversations without user re-entry. Any Office Claude can read/write bon items in its own document via Custom XML Parts. A bon skill uploaded to claude.ai teaches the workflow and includes the persistence code.

title: Verify Agent SDK API shape

brief.why: Two actions (aby-vaniho context-pressure reflector, aby-hiwuca memory flush) depend on PreCompact hooks existing in the Agent SDK. NanoClaw uses them, which is evidence, but we haven't confirmed the API directly. A Claude session can npm install the SDK, read the types, and confirm query(), hooks, session resume, and the PreCompact interface — preventing rework if the API is different from what we assume.
brief.what: 1. npm init + npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk in a scratch dir 2. Read the TypeScript type definitions for query(), hooks, PreCompact, session resume 3. Confirm: PreCompact hook exists and receives transcript_path and session_id 4. Confirm: query() accepts resume and systemPrompt options 5. Document actual API shape in docs/agent-sdk-api.md 6. Flag any mismatches with our assumptions
brief.done: docs/agent-sdk-api.md exists with confirmed API shapes for query(), PreCompact hook, and session resume. Any assumption mismatches are flagged and corresponding bons are updated.

done_note: Unnecessary — Gueridon already provides the spawn primitive via claude CLI + stream-json. No SDK needed.

title: Workers and reflectors operate obliviously with structured interfaces

brief.why: Workers and reflectors are the kitchen — they see a directory, a CLAUDE.md, and bons. They don't know they're orchestrated. The interfaces between them and the PM must be structured (JSON verdicts, named sessions, standardised handoffs) so the PM can route automatically without reading full transcripts.
brief.what: 1. Worker CLAUDE.md conventions — what workers see, how they write handoffs 2. Structured reflector verdict schema (JSON: approved/rejected/escalate, issues, confidence) 3. Session naming in handoffs and file deposits 4. Pre-compaction memory flush hook (from aby-hiwuca)
brief.done: Workers produce handoffs the PM can parse. Reflectors write structured JSON verdicts. Session names appear in all artifacts. Pre-compaction flush prevents context loss.

title: The aboyeur routes triggers with minimal context

brief.why: The aboyeur is a persistent, minimal-context Claude session that holds goals (bon outcomes) and routes triggers. It decides: one-shot for simple tasks, PM for multi-session projects. It detects promotion — when a one-shot creates bons, it spawns a PM to take over. It sees only summaries and escalations, never project details.
brief.what: 1. Aboyeur CLAUDE.md — goals, routing rules, promotion detection 2. One-shot vs PM routing logic (rewrite of aby-puvoni) 3. Promotion pattern: one-shot creates bons, aboyeur notices, spawns PM 4. Session naming convention — aboyeur assigns names, all tiers use them 5. Decision heuristics for unattended routing (from aby-lolero)
brief.done: Aboyeur runs persistently with minimal context consumption. Routes one-shot and PM correctly. Detects promotion from one-shot to PM. Session names are consistent and self-documenting.

title: Context-pressure reflector trigger

brief.why: No system has solved context compaction in long-running agents. Agent Teams fails catastrophically. The reflector pattern is the answer: when a worker nears compaction, spawn a reflector to summarize into a handoff, then start a fresh worker. This is compaction-as-review, not compaction-as-loss.
brief.what: 1. PreCompact hook in Claude Code settings.json (not SDK — CC hooks work for any claude process) 2. Hook writes a sentinel file that the daemon detects 3. Daemon spawns reflector with 'the previous worker is about to lose context — summarize and handoff' prompt 4. Reflector reads worker's partial handoff and recent tool output 5. Reflector writes full handoff with assessment, risks, and next steps 6. Daemon spawns fresh worker hydrated from that handoff
brief.done: A worker session that runs long enough to trigger compaction gets a reflector review. The next worker session picks up from the handoff with no context loss. Tested with a deliberately long-running task.

title: Worker Claudes get fresh-eyes review and context survives compaction

brief.why: Long-running agents lose context when compaction fires (Agent Teams loses team awareness entirely). Workers make mistakes that accumulate without review. The reflector pattern solves both — fresh eyes catch errors, and the handoff file preserves state across context boundaries.
brief.what: 1. Upgrade conductor.sh to spawn via Agent SDK (not shell adapter) 2. Add context-pressure trigger — spawn reflector when worker nears compaction limits 3. Reflector writes handoff that hydrates the next worker 4. Pre-compaction memory flush hook (agent saves critical state before summarization) 5. Planning reflector prompt for architecture review (not just code review)
brief.done: A worker session that runs for 45+ minutes gets a reflector review before context is lost. The next worker resumes seamlessly from the handoff. Planning-phase reflectors catch assumption errors like the 'mise polling needs a Claude' mistake.

done_note: Actions redistributed to aby-nafola (PM) and aby-sumode (workers). Fresh-eyes review is now the beat pattern managed by PM Claude.

title: HEARTBEAT.md cron trigger

brief.why: The HEARTBEAT cron serves two purposes: (1) periodic checks — review HEARTBEAT.md, run health checks, spot-check project state, and (2) aboyeur liveness — if the aboyeur died or a PM is stuck, the HEARTBEAT trigger wakes a fresh aboyeur that can detect and recover. Without it, a dead aboyeur means silence.
brief.what: 1. Cron trigger source in the daemon poll loop 2. HEARTBEAT.md checklist format (what to check each cycle) 3. Aboyeur liveness check: is the aboyeur session alive? is each PM progressing? 4. Configurable interval (default: 30 minutes)
brief.done: HEARTBEAT fires on schedule. Aboyeur is restarted if dead. Stuck PMs are detected and escalated.

title: End-to-end test harness

brief.why: Carlini's key insight: environment-as-prompt means tests matter more than prompt sophistication. The daemon's full cycle (trigger → queue → spawn → agent work → output → route) needs an automated test that doesn't require a real Gmail account or Max subscription. Mock the boundaries, test the plumbing.
brief.what: 1. Test SQLite trigger insertion and dedup 2. Test queue ordering and concurrency limits 3. Test spawnAgent with a mock Agent SDK (return canned output) 4. Test full cycle: mock trigger → queue → mock spawn → parse output → verify routing 5. Test crash recovery: kill daemon mid-cycle, restart, verify pending triggers are recovered
brief.done: npm test passes. Full cycle test runs in <5 seconds with no external dependencies. Crash recovery test verified.

steps: Test SQLite trigger insertion and dedup
Test queue ordering and concurrency limits
Test spawnAgent with a mock Agent SDK (return canned output)
Test full cycle: mock trigger → queue → mock spawn → parse output → verify routing
Test crash recovery: kill daemon mid-cycle, restart, verify pending triggers are recovered

title: Explore-phase and planning-phase prompt pairs

brief.why: The explore→plan transition needs worker/reflector prompts for each phase. Explore workers research options; explore reflectors challenge assumptions. Plan workers convert direction to Bons; plan reflectors check Bons against research. These are the prompts that encode what Sameer did in this conversation.
brief.what: 1. shared/prompts/explore-worker.md — research, build options, sketch architectures 2. shared/prompts/explore-reflector.md — challenge assumptions, walk through data flows, find hidden decisions 3. shared/prompts/plan-worker.md — convert approved direction to Bon outcomes and actions 4. shared/prompts/plan-reflector.md — check Bons against research, verify completeness 5. Phase transition criteria in each reflector prompt
brief.done: Running explore-worker then explore-reflector on a new problem produces a clear options summary with challenged assumptions. Running plan-worker then plan-reflector produces well-formed Bons with all three flags populated.

title: Incoming email gets triaged and drafted without human involvement

brief.why: First concrete use case for the daemon. Validates the full stack: trigger detection, agent spawning, tool usage (mise), response routing. Email is high-value — saves daily time — and draft-only mode is safe.
brief.what: 1. OAuth credentials for claude@planetmodha.com via jeton 2. Add send operation to mise-en-space (locked behind explicit flag) 3. Email triage prompt template (classify, draft reply or escalate) 4. Gmail poll trigger in daemon (direct API, not via mise MCP) 5. Thread context via per-thread handoff files
brief.done: Email to claude@planetmodha.com gets a draft reply in Gmail within 5 minutes. Human reviews and sends. Escalation emails trigger a notification via Gueridon.

done_note: Actions redistributed to aby-hibusa (daemon, OAuth) and aby-poniha (email pipeline). Email is now a cross-cutting concern, not a standalone outcome.

title: Triggers spawn Claude agents without human initiation

brief.why: Currently every Claude session requires a human to start it. For autonomous email triage, scheduled work, and event-driven responses, the system needs to react to external triggers (Gmail, cron, webhooks) and spawn agents independently.
brief.what: A thin persistent daemon (~700 lines Node/Python) that: 1. Polls external triggers via native APIs (not MCP) 2. Normalizes triggers into a SQLite queue 3. Drains the queue through a per-context FIFO with concurrency limits 4. Spawns Claude sessions via Agent SDK with Gueridon's env-var stripping pattern 5. Parses output markers and routes responses
brief.done: Daemon runs as a systemd/launchd service. A new email to claude@planetmodha.com results in a Claude session being spawned within 60 seconds, with no human involvement.

done_note: Actions redistributed to aby-hibusa (daemon) and aby-titugu (aboyeur). Architecture shifted from 'triggers spawn agents' to 'daemon wakes aboyeur which routes.'
